<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage.qmd@062c1f13a
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage

The previous chapter made planning visible before execution. This chapter asks
what happens next. Once a goal has been accepted and shaped into a plan, the
system still has to decide who should do each part, what order the work should
follow, how much context each step deserves, when a cheap route is good enough,
and when the work has to escalate to something stronger.

PlanForge is the book's name for that scheduling layer. It treats a plan as a
directed acyclic graph: a set of work nodes whose dependencies are explicit and
whose routes are accountable. A node is not just a task label. It is a bounded
piece of cognitive labor with a required input state, an authority boundary, a
context request, a capability tier, an adequacy test, a verifier, a budget, and
a failure path.

That makes intelligence arbitrage possible without turning efficiency into a
race to the bottom. The stack can use a small model, local worker, tool, cached
artifact, specialist core, or human reviewer when that route is adequate. It
can also spend more when the graph shows that risk, uncertainty, context demand,
or downstream cost requires it. The point is not cheap cognition. The point is
priced, routed, and verified cognition.

In a governed stack, the cheapest acceptable route is allowed only because the
acceptance predicate remains attached. A cheaper worker that pushes verification
or cleanup onto somebody else has not saved money. It has moved the bill.

## Problem

Large tasks do not usually fail because they lack a single brilliant answer.
They fail because the work is not decomposed, sequenced, routed, checked, and
merged with enough care. A book chapter, software change, safety review, model
evaluation, or research program contains different kinds of labor. Some parts
are clerical. Some require search. Some require proof. Some require source
reading. Some require human judgment. Some should not proceed until authority,
context, or budget is available.

A flat prompt hides those differences. A chat transcript can say "do the
project," but it does not show which work can run in parallel, which work is
blocked, which worker is allowed to act, which output is acceptable, or which
failed path should leave a residual. A governed AI system needs a scheduling
surface where those facts are explicit.

PlanForge-style DAG planning supplies that surface. It takes the plan graph and
turns it into scheduled, typed work. The graph exposes dependencies, critical
paths, parallel branches, merge points, cached work, route choices, verification
burdens, and residuals. The result is not yet a deployed scheduler in this
repository. It is an architectural contract for what a scheduler must record
before the book can honestly claim cost savings or quality-preserving routing.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

The simplest mistake is to send every node to the strongest available model.
That can work, but it wastes compute and hides structure. If every task receives
the same expensive treatment, the system never learns which parts were easy,
which parts needed proof, which parts needed human review, or which cheap route
would have been adequate with the right verifier.

The opposite mistake is to route too cheaply. A weak worker can produce an
answer that looks plausible while missing a dependency, source constraint,
authority ceiling, or quality predicate. The schedule looks efficient until the
repair, review, and human cleanup costs arrive later. That is not intelligence
arbitrage; it is accounting failure.

Classical and agentic planning literature already shows why explicit structure
matters. PDDL and SHOP2 separate planning domains, tasks, actions, and
decomposition methods. Task and motion planning names the boundary between
symbolic plans and physical feasibility. Behavior trees and GOAP make modular
and reactive control visible. Tree of Thoughts exposes the value of searching
over intermediate reasoning paths. PlanForge sits beside those ideas rather
than replacing them. Its added emphasis is costed route selection under
authority, context, adequacy, verification, and residual constraints.

Serial chat orchestration also hides merge risk. Two independent branches can
produce artifacts that sound compatible while relying on different assumptions,
source versions, permission scopes, or acceptance criteria. A DAG should force
the merge condition into the open before parallelism becomes incoherence.

## Core Claim

PlanForge-style DAG planning can support intelligence arbitrage by assigning
each node the minimum adequate capability and verification burden. This remains
an architectural argument, not a measured result.

The claim has several parts. PlanForge and the PlanForge compiler-architecture
note supply the decomposition, primitive typing, dependency-aware scheduling,
minimum viable intelligence, fallback, and planner-as-compiler frame.
Cognitive Compilation connects the graph to semantic IR, local repair, and
validator-backed artifacts. TokenMana keeps the resource surface wider than
token count by adding load, latency, review capacity, burst limits, and human
cognitive friction. The Coherence Exchange contributes speculative
intelligence-arbitrage language and verification-supply-chain context, but it
does not provide implemented economics. MoECOT remains implementation-reference
context only until runtime artifacts, logs, or benchmark records are imported
and checked.

The live book's support state therefore stays at `argument`. The available repo
evidence supports record shape, finite proof hooks, and synthetic consistency
checks. It does not prove scheduler behavior, route-selection quality, cost
savings, or benchmark performance.

## Mechanism

PlanForge begins by decomposing a goal into primitive nodes. Each node receives
dependencies, inputs, expected outputs, context demand, authority state,
capability tier, quality predicate, verification requirement, budget limit,
route assignment, merge condition, escalation path, and residual behavior.

That extra structure is what changes the meaning of "use a smaller model." The
small model is not trusted because it is small or cheap. It is trusted only
when its node has a bounded job, an adequacy contract, a verifier, and a
fallback. The larger model, human reviewer, or specialist route is not used
because it is prestigious. It is used because the graph shows that the cheaper
route would not satisfy the node's acceptance conditions.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Accepted plan"] --> B["Primitive work nodes"]
  B --> C["Dependency DAG"]
  C --> D["Capability and context annotations"]
  D --> E["Adequacy contract"]
  E --> F["Route assignment"]
  F --> G{"Predicate passes?"}
  G -- "yes" --> H["Merge or cache artifact"]
  G -- "no" --> I["Escalate or residualize"]
  H --> J["Cost-quality ledger"]
  I --> J
```

The diagram is not a benchmark. It shows the accountability path. A planned
node becomes schedulable only after its dependencies and annotations are
visible. A routed node becomes acceptable only after its adequacy predicate is
checked. A failed node does not disappear; it escalates or leaves a residual.
The ledger records what was accepted, rejected, merged, retried, or left
blocked.

Every node should carry two contracts. The work contract says what the node is
allowed to do. The adequacy contract says what would make the output usable by
downstream dependents. Without the second contract, scheduling creates text but
not reliable progress. A worker can complete a task description while still
failing the actual need of the graph.

The DAG also makes local repair possible. If a downstream artifact fails
because one source-reading node used stale evidence, the scheduler should not
regenerate the entire project. It should identify the affected subgraph,
repair the stale node, rerun dependents whose assumptions changed, and preserve
unaffected work. That is the compiler intuition behind PlanForge: the graph
lets the stack rebuild only the parts whose dependencies changed.

## Interfaces

PlanForge exposes a DAG to the rest of the stack.

Routing consumes the capability annotations and route assignments. VCM consumes
the context requests. Command contracts define authority and acceptance
boundaries. Cognitive Compilation lowers scheduled work into semantic atoms,
target constraints, validators, and repair ledgers. Evidence records cost,
quality, residuals, and verification results.

The minimum record is deliberately concrete. It needs a `dag_id`, a
`parent_plan`, nodes, edges, scheduling states, capability tiers, context
requests, adequacy contracts, quality predicates, verification requirements,
budget limits, route assignments, merge conditions, assumption references,
escalation policy, residuals, a cost-quality ledger, and non-claims.

Scheduling state matters because "not done" is too vague. A node may be ready,
blocked by dependency, blocked by authority, blocked by context, blocked by
budget, blocked by verifier, running, failed, residualized, or merged. Those
states prevent blocked work from being mistaken for available work and prevent
failed cheap routes from being hidden behind a final success label.

## Invariants

The graph must respect dependency order. A node cannot be ready just because an
upstream worker produced text; it becomes ready when the upstream artifact,
authority state, context state, and quality predicate satisfy the downstream
need or when an explicit governance waiver exists.

Cost savings must not remove required verification. If the verification burden
is moved outside the ledger, the route has not become cheaper in a meaningful
sense. It has only made the accounting worse.

Escalation paths must be explicit. A failed quality predicate should route to a
stronger worker, human review, repair, quarantine, or residual. Silent success
after a failed predicate is the failure case the graph is meant to prevent.

The current Lean hooks prove only narrow finite-record statements around these
ideas: a dispatchable indexed plan graph carries an acyclicity certificate and
ordered dependency edges, and a failed quality predicate must escalate or emit
a residual under the valid node-outcome predicate. Those proofs are useful
guardrails, not a deployed scheduler proof.

## Failure Modes

Wrong tier selection produces cheap wrong work. The route looks efficient until
the downstream validator fails or a human has to repair the missing reasoning.

Dependency cycles make the graph look planned while it cannot dispatch. A
cycle is not a clever dependency. It is a stalled schedule that must be
rejected or repaired before execution.

Economic optimization can override safety if the scheduler is rewarded for
lower apparent cost while ignoring verification, repair, review, and residual
burden. This is the planning version of Goodhart pressure: the metric improves
while the system gets less trustworthy.

Merge laundering is another failure. Parallel branches may each pass locally
while disagreeing about assumptions. If the merge condition does not compare
source versions, authority ceilings, quality predicates, and residuals, the DAG
can turn parallelism into false coherence.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is not a smart planner. It is a public
PlanForge DAG record, schema validation, negative controls, and a small
cross-record harness that checks consistency with command contracts, plan
graphs, semantic atoms, typed jobs, approvals, and dispatch receipts.

The repository already has that kind of narrow surface: `schemas/planforge_dag`
defines the record fields, and the plan-execution contract harness checks
synthetic valid and invalid fixtures. The recorded local result passed two
valid fixtures and rejected five expected-invalid fixtures covering a cyclic
DAG, contract mismatch, unreceipted dispatch, requirement loss, and approval
bypass.

That is useful, but the boundary matters. The harness checks record consistency
in synthetic examples. It is not a route-selection benchmark, deployed
scheduler, parser-quality result, runtime adapter test, tool-execution trace,
or proof of AI behavior. The PlanForge experiment directory still records that
planning and control experiments are planned, not implemented or run.

The next practical slice should be a scheduler-state fixture set: one ready
node, one node blocked by missing context, one cheap route rejected because the
adequacy predicate fails, one branch merge blocked by conflicting assumptions,
and one local repair that touches only the affected subgraph. Passing that
fixture would still not prove performance, but it would make the scheduling
semantics sharper.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature version is a governed scheduler and capacity allocator for cognitive
work. It does not merely draw a DAG. It turns plan nodes into schedulable units
with dependency order, context demand, authority state, capability tier,
adequacy contract, verifier, cost envelope, route assignment, merge condition,
escalation path, and residual behavior.

In that mature system, intelligence arbitrage is measured after verification,
repair, merge, human review, and residual cost are counted. A cheap route only
counts as adequate when its output satisfies the node's adequacy contract and
remains useful to downstream dependents. An expensive route is justified when
the risk, context burden, quality predicate, or failure cost demands it. The
scheduler is therefore accountable for quality and authority, not just token
spend.

The beyond-state-of-the-art version would learn from its own ledgers without
laundering evidence. It would know which node types were safely handled by
small local routes, which required stronger reasoning, which depended on
human review, which failed because context was insufficient, and which route
choices later caused expensive repairs. It would use that memory to improve
future routing while still preserving approval boundaries, source provenance,
and residual accounting.

That is the logical endpoint. It is not the current repo state. The book should
not claim it until there are scheduler implementations, route-selection traces,
adequacy tests, cost-quality ledgers, fallback records, and reproduced
comparisons.

## What Is Proven, And What Is Not

The current project proves or checks only narrow things around PlanForge:
schema shape, synthetic cross-record consistency, and finite Lean predicates
for dependency ordering and failed-quality fallback. These are the beginning of
an evidence lane.

They do not prove selected-tier adequacy, cost savings, model-routing quality,
planner decomposition quality, dependency inference, deployed scheduler cycle
rejection, runtime replanning, tool execution, MoECOT runtime behavior,
AutoGen-style agent performance, or external benchmark performance.

This distinction is important because PlanForge is attractive precisely where
overclaiming is easy. A diagram can make a plan look rigorous. A cost column can
make a route look efficient. A success state can hide failed branches. The
evidence discipline of the ASI Stack says those surfaces count only when the
residuals, failures, verification burden, and non-claims stay attached.

## Summary

PlanForge turns planning into schedulable cognitive labor. It decomposes work,
orders dependencies, annotates capability and context needs, routes each node,
checks adequacy, escalates failures, and records cost-quality outcomes.

Its central idea is simple: spend intelligence where the graph says it is
needed, and save it only where the acceptance predicate proves that a cheaper
route was enough. A governed DAG is therefore not a shortcut around evidence.
It is a way of making evidence, cost, and route choice visible in the same
place.

The next chapter moves from scheduled work to compiled artifacts. Cognitive
Compilation and Semantic IR explains how plan obligations become semantic
atoms, validators, target constraints, lowering receipts, and repair ledgers so
the stack can audit what survived the translation from plan to artifact.
